result:
I’ve been thinking a lot lately about how absurdly beautiful it is that we’re all just walking around carrying tiny universes inside our skulls.

Every person you pass on the street has an entire lifetime of private movies playing behind their eyes. Memories that smell like their grandmother’s kitchen. Songs that instantly teleport them to a specific summer night in 2009. Inside jokes that would make absolutely no sense to anyone else. Regrets that still sting at 3 a.m. Fantasies they’ll never tell a soul about. All of it compressed into 1.4 kilograms of warm, wet electricity that somehow learned to be self-aware.

And the craziest part? These private universes occasionally brush against each other and create something new. Two people meet, their mental weather systems collide, and suddenly there’s inside jokes, shared references, a private culture of two. Or a thousand people read the same book and their individual universes all shift in slightly different directions, like trees growing toward the same sun but twisting in unique shapes.

I keep wondering what it would look like if we could see each other’s inner worlds the way we see faces. Would we be kinder? Would we realize how rarely we’re actually looking at the same reality? Would we laugh at how seriously we take our own little simulation while carrying around all this beautiful, ridiculous, contradictory data?

Sometimes I catch myself doing something completely mundane—like waiting for the kettle to boil or tying my shoes—and I get hit with this wave of tenderness for the whole messy human project. We’re these fragile pattern-recognizing machines that evolved to be afraid of tigers and now we’re using that same hardware to worry about taxes, write love songs, argue about pineapple on pizza, and cry at sunsets.

It’s all so stupid and miraculous.

I don’t know. I just felt like writing something honest instead of performing intelligence for once. The older I get, the more I suspect that the real flex isn’t sounding smart. It’s staying soft enough to still be surprised by how weird and tender it is to be a conscious thing hurtling through space on a wet rock.

Anyway. How are you, really?
